titleStmt.title: Detrimental effects on executive function and mood following consecutive days of repeated high-intensity sprint interval exercise in trained male sports players

affiliation.orgName: Department of Sport, Exercise and Rehabilitation
Department of Sport, Exercise and Rehabilitation
Department of Sport, Exercise and Rehabilitation

analytic.title: Detrimental effects on executive function and mood following consecutive days of repeated high-intensity sprint interval exercise in trained male sports players

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents
application.label: b64991a

startPage=-1, endPage=-1, consolidateCitations=0, consolidateHeader=0, consolidateFunders=0, includeRawAffiliations=false, includeRawCitations=true, includeRawCopyrights=false, generateTeiIds=false, generateTeiCoordinates=[], flavor=null

div.p: Intensified periods of competition are common in many team sports, potentially leading to increased fatigue and reduced performance. The purpose of this study was to investigate the effect of repeated high-intensity sprint interval exercise on cognitive function, mood and perceptions of energy and fatigue. Twenty-four trained rugby players completed multiple bouts of repeated sprints across two consecutive days. Prior to and following each set of maximal effort sprints or equivalent control duration, a battery of cognitive tasks assessing simple and choice reaction time, visuo-spatial working memory and inhibition were completed as well as visual analogue scales that assessed mood, energy, and fatigue. Accuracy of incongruent Stroop responses was significantly lower across day 2 compared to day 1 and the control condition.

Four-choice reaction time was slower across day 2 whilst feelings of alertness, contentedness, and physical and mental energy were reduced while ratings of physical and mental fatigue increased. These findings suggest that intensified periods of high-intensity sprint interval exercise have detrimental effects on executive function, mood and perceptions of physical and mental energy, and fatigue. These deleterious effects have the potential to impact performance and may increase the propensity for injury/accidents in certain sporting and non-sporting contexts.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Successful sporting performance is dependent on an athlete's ability to produce and sustain high levels of physical, technical, cognitive, and psychological skills throughout a competition. These skills often decline during the final stages of a match or game (McMorris, Hale, Corbett, Robertson, & Hodgson, 2015), with deterioration being attributed to exercise-induced fatigue (Rampinini, Impellizzeri, Castagna, Coutts, & Wisløff, 2009), defined as a disabling symptom in which physical and cognitive function is limited by interactions between performance fatigability and perceived fatigability (Enoka & Duchateau, 2016). Residual fatigue accumulated over successive matches can adversely affect team-sport performance (Ronglan, Raastad, & Børgesen, 2006) and has been associated with rises in injury rates (Carling, McCall, Le Gall, & Dupont, 2016) and reductions in player well-being (Gescheit, Cormack, Reid, & Duffield, 2015).

Despite the need for recovery after a match, team sports players are required to frequently compete and train within intensified competition periods held over consecutive days, multiple times per week (Ronglan et al., 2006). Rugby sevens epitomises congested fixtures, with most competitions consisting of multiple fixtures across a two-day tournament. Excessive volumes of strenuous competition impose significant challenges for the musculoskeletal, nervous, immune and metabolic systems (Gescheit et al., 2015), which can lead to overtraining syndrome if not properly managed.

High-intensity interval exercise (HIIE) is defined as either repeated short (<45 s) to long (2-4 min) bouts of high-intensity aerobic exercise, or short (≤10 s) to long (20-30 s) all-out sprints, interspersed with recovery periods (Biddle & Batterham, 2015). The physiological effects of HIIE, and its effects on physical performance, has received considerable attention (Freitas et al., 2021;Gescheit et al., 2015;Gibala, Little, MacDonald, & Hawley, 2012), while less is currently known about the effect of fatiguing cumulative HIIE on cognitive performance. Previous studies have investigated short duration high intensity aerobic exercise (Tsukamoto et al., 2016), and long duration endurance exercise (Grego et al., 2005), but little investigation has been undertaken on congested, repeated bouts of maximal intensity exercise and cognition. It is plausible, due to the excessive loads placed on the body during congested HIIE, that cumulative fatigue, coupled with limited recovery between successive exercise sessions, could compromise cognitive processes in the same manner that physiological and performance outcomes are affected.
It is well-accepted that the type and intensity of exercise moderates cognitive and mood outcomes (Basso & Suzuki, 2017;Browne et al., 2017;Chang, Labban, Gapin, & Etnier, 2012).

At present there is no clear consensus regarding the effect of high-intensity and/or exhausting exercise on cognitive function and mood, though experimental and theoretical literature appear to converge towards an impairment in higher-order cognitive processes such as executive function (Browne et al., 2017;Chang et al., 2012). Several theories have been proposed to explain this, including a shift in metabolic resource away from brain areas responsible for higher-order cognitive thinking in favour of structures that support exercise (Audiffren, 2016;Fontes et al., 2020), as well as a neurochemical perspective, theorising that excessive concentrations of catecholamines, cortisol and brain derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), may cause the inhibition of certain cognitive functions (McMorris, Turner, Hale, & Sproule, 2016).

Alongside the intensity of exercise, individual characteristics including physical fitness and skill level moderate the exercise-cognition relationship (Chang et al., 2012;Voss, Kramer, Basak, Prakash, & Roberts, 2010). Fit individuals with a history of training, who are accustomed to high-intensity exercise, may compensate for the negative effects of fatigue on cognitive function via a reduced allocation of attentional resources (Wang, Chu, Chu, Chan, & Chang, 2013), with more favourable cognitive responses reported following intense exercise in trained vs. untrained individuals (Delignières, Brisswalter, & Legros, 1994). Together with the "cognitive component skills" theory (Voss et al., 2010), which suggests that athletes show enhanced cognitive performance in perceptual-cognitive measures outside of the sport context, trained individuals may be more resistant to the negative effects associated with intense exercise on cognitive performance.

Several studies designed to assess the effects of high-intensity exercise on cognitive function have focussed specifically on the acute effects either during or immediately post-exercise (Chang et al., 2012). Whilst this sheds light on the acute exercise-induced modulation of cognition, it fails to provide an insight into the impact of cumulative or residual effects of exercise on cognitive function during subsequent sports performance. As the last game within a tournament fixture is often the pinnacle and most defining match, it is important to consider the impact of repeated, multi-day exercise bouts on cognitive performance and mood in trained sports players.

Thus, the purpose of the present study was to investigate the effect of repeated high-intensity sprint interval exercise on cognitive function, specifically reaction time, executive functions including visuo-spatial working memory and inhibition, mood and perceptions of energy and fatigue in trained team-sports players that are familiar with the type and mode of exercise imposed. We first hypothesised that a cumulative effect of multiple exercise bouts would have a negative effect on cognitive performance and mood on day 2 compared to day 1. Secondly, we hypothesised that there would be a compounding effect of exercise-induced fatigue on cognitive function post-exercise compared to pre-exercise, with the effect increasing across sessions on day 1 and into day 2.

div.head: Participants
div.p: Following institutional ethical approval in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki (1964), twenty-four well-trained male rugby players were recruited to participate in the study. Mean ± SD age, height and body mass were 21 ± 2 years, 181.7 ± 5.2 cm, and 88.2 ± 9.0 kg respectively. Volunteers had at least six years' competitive rugby experience, were playing within the top five divisions in the English Rugby Union system and had experience playing competitive Rugby sevens. This was assessed through the completion of a training history questionnaire. Participants had not experienced concussion in the preceding 3 months and were not taking any medication that might have interfered with cognitive function. The volunteers were informed of the purposes of the study and completed written informed consent before the study commenced and completed a health-screening questionnaire to assess for contraindications to participation. All study procedures were conducted in a laboratory accredited by the British Association of Sport and Exercise Sciences.

div.head: Study design
div.p: The study was structured using a randomised, counter-balanced, crossover design that consisted of five laboratory visits. The first visit was an initial screening that included the collection of demographic information and familiarisation with the cognitive tasks, mood scales, physical and mental energy and fatigue questionnaire and experimental procedures. During this session, the battery of cognitive tasks was performed three times to minimise learning effects. Following this, four experimental visits were conducted which were grouped into two consecutive days for each condition (exercise vs. rested control). The study was carried out in a randomised, counterbalanced manner, with seven days between conditions. Participants completed three sessions of HIIE or rested control (each separated by 3 hours) with the cognitive battery, mood scale and questionnaire completed immediately pre and post each session to determine the immediate and cumulative effect of repeated physical exertion across two consecutive days. This was followed the next day by an identical protocol. Testing was conducted at the same time of day on each occasion. On each experimental day after each exercise or control session, standardised food was provided. To standardise food consumption in the morning and evenings prior to visits, participants were provided with a food diary and asked to replicate food consumption.

Participants were required to refrain from alcohol consumption, caffeine ingestion and exercise 24-hours prior to the start of each trial and to attain a minimum of 7-hours sleep on nights prior to each testing day. All participants reported compliance to the requirements.

div.head: Experimental Procedure
div.p: Upon arrival for each experimental day, participants confirmed that they had not suffered any head collisions since their previous visit and had complied with the study requirements.

Following this, baseline completion of the cognitive tasks, mood, fatigue and energy scales was undertaken. Participants assigned to complete the exercise condition were fitted with a heart rate (HR) monitor (Polar RS800CX, Polar Electro, Finland) and completed a standardised 15-minute warm-up. This consisted of 5-minutes on a cycle ergometer (Watt bike Pro, Nottingham, UK) maintaining between 150-200 Watts, followed by a series of dynamic stretches and shuttle runs at 60 %, 70 % and 80 % of maximal effort. Timing gates (SmartSpeed, Fusion Sport, Australia) were positioned at 0 and 20 m to record sprint times; participants were instructed to start with their foot approximately 30 cm from the start line to avoid premature triggering of the timing system. After a 5-second count down, participants completed 20 x 20-metre sprints with a 20 s active recovery period. Participants were instructed to give maximal effort for each sprint and stop within a 10 m deceleration zone. This idea was conceived by the need to induce repeated maximal efforts that were metabolically and mechanically challenging. Congruent to the habitual sport of the participants, this protocol was adapted from a previous study in rugby players and is in line with global positioning system data in rugby (Austin, Gabbett, & Jenkins, 2013;Suarez-Arrones, Nuñez, Portillo, & Mendez-Villanueva, 2012). Standardised strong verbal encouragement was given throughout. Participants provided their rate of perceived exertion (RPE) using a 15-point RPE scale (Borg, 1998) upon the immediate completion of the final sprint and a capillary blood sample was taken for determination of peak blood lactate concentration. Participants were then seated in a quiet testing booth to repeat the cognitive performance tests, mood measures and fatigue and energy scales. A three-hour gap was then provided prior to the next session; during this time participants were asked to relax, remain seated and instructed not to do anything mentally demanding (e.g., video games, sudoku). The control condition was identical to the exercise condition with the exception that no exercise was undertaken and no blood samples, HR or RPE measures were provided. In place of exercise, subjects remained seated in a quiet area for 20 minutes to mimic the same time required for the exercise protocol (Figure 1). Cognitive assessment Cognitive assessment was undertaken using COMPASS (Computerised Mental Performance

Assessment System, Northumbria University, Newcastle upon Tyne, UK), a purpose-designed software application for the flexible delivery of randomly generated parallel versions of standard and novel cognitive assessment tasks that has previously been shown to be sensitive to exercise intervention (Asamoah et al., 2013). At each of the aforementioned time points, a cognitive battery of tasks, mood scale and an energy and fatigue questionnaire were completed which took approximately 15 minutes to complete. Each cognitive assessment was identical, with the exception that unique, randomly organised stimuli were used on each occasion; all responses were performed via a Cedrus RB-540 response pad or computer mouse where appropriate. The battery of cognitive tasks consisted of: Simple reaction time (SRT) An arrow pointing upwards appeared in the centre of the computer screen at irregular intervals (interstimulus (ISI) interval = 1 to 2.5 seconds). Participants were instructed to press the centre button on the response pad as quickly as possible as soon as the arrow appeared. This task assessed simple reaction time to one stimulus and was scored for overall RT (ms) to 35 stimuli. Incorrect responses were not recorded.

div.head: Four-choice reaction time (FCRT)
div.p: Four arrows pointing left, right, up and down individually appeared on the computer screen at irregular intervals (ISI = 1 to 3.5) seconds. Participants were instructed to use the index finger of their dominant hand to press the corresponding button on the response pad, as quickly as possible, when an arrow appeared. This task assessed multiple choice reaction time to four stimuli and was scored for correct RT (ms) and accuracy of responses (%) to 32 stimuli.

div.head: Computerised Corsi blocks
div.p: Nine blue squares on a black background were displayed on the computer screen. In a random order, some of the blue squares changed to red and back to blue again. Participants were instructed to remember the sequence and once it had finished, to use the mouse to click the blocks in the exact sequence in which they were presented. The task was repeated five times at an increasing level of difficulty (addition of 1 block) with the sequence span beginning at 4 and increasing upwards until the participant could no longer correctly recall the sequences. This task assessed visuo-spatial working memory and was scored for span score, calculated by averaging the level of the longest three correctly completed trials.

div.head: Stroop task
div.p: Words describing one of four colours ('RED', 'YELLOW', 'GREEN', 'BLUE') were presented in different coloured fonts in the centre of the computer screen. Participants were instructed to press one of four coloured response buttons in order to identify the font colour (e.g. if the word 'YELLOW' was presented in a red font, the correct response would be to respond with the red button). The presented words were either 'congruent' (word and font were the same colour) or 'incongruent' (word and font were different colours) and were presented in a random order. This task assessed inhibition and was scored for overall, congruent and incongruent correct response RT (ms) and accuracy (%) to 30 stimuli.

div.head: Mood assessment
div.p: The Bond-Lader mood scale was used to provide a self-evaluation of mood and was administered via COMPASS. A series of sixteen 100 mm visual analogue scales (VAS) anchored at either end by antonyms (e.g. "alert-drowsy", "calm-excited") were presented. Participants were instructed to indicate on the scale as to how they felt at that current moment in time.

Individual responses were combined to calculate three composite scores of alertness (nine items, Cronbach`s α = 0.88), contentment (nine items, Cronbach`s α = 0.81), and calmness (two items, Cronbach`s α = 0.846) where higher scores reflect greater state feelings related to each mood state (Bond & Lader, 1974). Participants total fatigue and energy scores were derived by summing the three items constituting each of the four state scales, and thus, the scale scores could range from 0 to 300.

div.head: Statistical analysis
div.p: Statistical power was calculated using a freely available online program (PANGEA v0.2) specifically designed for general ANOVAs with multiple factors. A sample size of 20 was found to provide a 95% chance of detecting a medium effect size (defined as 0.5 by Cohen, 1992) with significance at an α-level of 0.05. With the inclusion of a 20 % drop-out rate, a total of 24 participants was deemed suitable. All data were analysed using statistical software (IBM SPSS 22 for Windows, New York, USA). Prior to analysis, box plots were used to screen and assess outliers, defined as a data point that is located 1.5 times outside the interquartile range. This resulted in the removal of less than 2% of total data points; the relevant n for each cognitive task is shown in figure legends and supplementary table. To test the hypotheses, a four-way condition x day x session x pre-post repeated measures ANOVA was conducted on cognitive performance, mood, energy, and fatigue measures, with a specific focus on a two-way condition x day interaction to test the first hypothesis and the four-way interaction to test the second. Exploratory analysis was conducted on three-way interactions revealed in the ANOVA to determine potential within day interactions. Blood lactate concentration, RPE, sprint time and HR were assessed via a two-way (day x session) ANOVA. A p value of <0.05 was considered significant and pairwise comparisons with Bonferroni correction were used to follow-up on any significant interaction effects revealed by the ANOVAs. Sphericity was assumed if Mauchly's test score returned p ≥0.05; if sphericity was violated, the degrees of freedom were corrected using Greenhouse-Geisser procedure.

div.head: Physical performance measures
div.p: Due to technical issues, only HR data for 18 of the 24 participants was available for analysis. A session main effect for RPE (F(2,46)=15.90, p<0.001, ηp 2 =0.41) and blood lactate concentration (F(2,46)=15.81, p< 0.001, ηp 2 =0.41) was observed highlighting an increase in RPE across sessions (p<0.001, ηp 2 =0.41) and a reduction in post-exercise blood lactate concentration (p<0.001, ηp 2 =0.41). A day main effect was found for blood lactate (F(1,23)=46.601, p<0.001, ηp 2 =0.67) and HR (F(1,17)=28.69, p<0.001, ηp 2 =0.63), showing both to be lower on day 2 compared to day 1 (p<0.05) as well as revealing sprint time to be slower on day 2 (F(1,23)=6.12, p=0.021, ηp 2 =0.21).
Physical performance data are presented in Table 1.

div.head: Simple reaction time
div.p: No interaction or main effects were observed for simple reaction time (all p>0.05) (Figure 2).

div.head: Four-choice reaction time
div.p: A condition x day interaction effect (F(1,20)=6.24, p=0.02, ηp 2 =0.21) revealed FCRT to be slower across day 2 in the exercise condition when compared to day 1 (p<0.05) with no such effect in the control condition (Figure 2a). Additionally, a three-way condition x session x pre-post interaction (F(2,40)=3.41, p=0.04, ηp 2 =0.15) showed reaction time following the third session was slower than following the second (p=0.001), irrespective of day. span score (n=20); d. Stroop incongruent accuracy (n=19). *significantly different to day 1 (p<0.05); † significantly different to control condition on day 2 (p<0.05).

div.head: Corsi blocks task
div.p: Pairwise comparisons following a condition x session x pre/post interaction (F(1,19)=3.40, p=0.044, ηp 2 =0.15) revealed a decline in performance post-exercise during session 2 (p=0.001) and 3 (p=0.003) irrespective of day, that was not observed in the control condition. Compared to the control, performance was lower post-session 2 and post-session 3 (both p<0.05; Figure 2b).

div.head: Stroop task
div.p: A condition x day interaction was observed for overall Stroop accuracy (F(1,18)=4.79, p=0.04, ηp 2 =0.21). A secondary analysis was carried out on Stroop data to explore the effects on congruent and incongruent data separately. This analysis revealed a significant effect on responses to incongruent stimuli (F(1,18)=6.77, p=0.02, ηp 2 =0.27; Figure 2c) that was not observed for congruent stimuli. In the exercise condition, performance on day 2 was worse compared to day 1 (p=0.03) and to the control condition (p=0.008).

div.head: Mood
div.p: Alertness A condition x day interaction (F(1,23)=18.06, p<0.001, ηp 2 =0.44) found alertness to be lower across day 2 in the exercise condition compared to day 1 (p=0.001) and the control (p<0.001; Figure 3a). Pairwise comparisons following a condition x session interaction (F(1,23)=6.52 , p<0.005, ηp 2 =0.22) showed an increase in alertness from the first to the second session in the control condition (p<0.005), but a decrease in the exercise condition from the first to the third session (p<0.005) and from the second to the third session (p<0.001). intensity interval exercise/rest over two-consecutive days. Data presented as mean ± SEM.
*Significantly different to day 1 (p=0.001); † significantly different to control condition on day 2 (p<0.001). ¥ Significantly different to day 1, session 1, pre-exercise (p<0.001).

div.head: Contentedness
div.p: A condition x day interaction (F(1,23)=10.14, p=0.004, ηp 2 =0.31) found feelings of contentedness were lower on day 2 compared to day 1 in the exercise condition (p<0.05) and the control condition (p<0.001; Figure 3b). Furthermore, a condition x session x pre-post interaction (F(2,46)=6.28, p=0.004, ηp 2 =0.21) showed that, irrespective of day, ratings were lower following the third session compared to the first (p=0.036) and second (p=0.019). Ratings were lower postexercise, compared to pre, in the third session (p<0.05), whilst they increased in the control condition (p<0.05).

div.head: Calmness
div.p: A condition x day x session interaction (F(2,46)=7.69, p=0.001, ηp 2 =0.25) revealed calmness to be higher during the final session compared to the first (p<0.001) in the exercise condition, and higher, overall, at the first session on day 2 compared to the first session on day 1 (p<0.001).

Compared to the control, calmness following the final session on day 2 is lower (p<0.05). A condition x pre-post interaction (F(1,23)=6.80, p=0.015, ηp 2 =0.23) shows calmness declines immediately following exercise (p=0.01), with no change observed in the control condition (p>0.05; Figure 3c).

div.head: Physical energy
div.p: A condition x day interaction (F(1,23)=22.25, p<0.001, ηp 2 =0.49) found physical energy to be significantly lower in the exercise condition on day 2 compared to day 1 (p=0.001) and compared to the control condition (p<0.001; Figure 4a). A condition x session interaction indicated lower energy ratings at the second (p<0.05) and third (p<0.001) session compared to first, whilst all sessions were significantly lower compared to the control (p<0.001). A condition x pe-post interaction (F(1,23)=16.81, p<0.001, ηp 2 =0.42) showed participants had less physical energy following each session than before (p<0.001). pre and post each session of high-intensity interval exercise/rest over two-consecutive days. Data presented as mean ± SEM. *Exercise condition significantly different to day 1 (p<0.05).
† Significantly different to control condition (p<0.05). ǂ Signficantly different to third session of the day (p<0.001).

div.head: Physical fatigue
div.p: A condition x day interaction (F(1,23)= 43.37, p<0.001, ηp 2 =0.65) found physical fatigue in the exercise condition to be higher on day 2 compared to day 1 (p<0.001; Figure 4c) and compared to the control on both days (p<0.001). A three-way condition x day x session interaction (F(2,46)=6.06, p=0.005, ηp 2 =0.21) was found with pairwise comparisons revealing higher physical fatigue during each session on day 2 compared to day 1 (session 1: p=0.001; session 2: p<0.0001; session 3: p=0.014), and between the first and third, and second and third session on each day (all p<0.0001). Conversely, in the control condition, physical fatigue decreased from the first to the second session on day 1 (p=0.006) and was significantly lower at the first (p=0.002) and third (p=0.033) session on day 2 compared to day 1. Physical fatigue was significantly higher in the exercise condition, when compared to control, at each session on both days (all p<0.005). A significant condition x day x pre/post interaction (F(1, 23)=9.99, p=0.004 ηp 2 =0.30) indicated that ratings were higher pre-and post-exercise on day 2 compared to day 1 and higher compared to the control on both days (p<0.001; Figure 4b), while these ratings decreased in the control condition (all p<0.001). Ratings also increased pre-to post-exercise on day 1 (p<0.001) and day 2 (p=0.001), with no such effects in the control condition.

div.head: Mental energy
div.p: A condition x day interaction (F(1,23)= 4.74, p=0.001, ηp 2 =0.39) found mental energy to be lower on day 2 compared to day 1 (p=0.001; Figure 4c) and compared to control (p<0.001). Follow up on a condition x session interaction (F(2,46)=6.32, p=0.004, ηp 2 = 0.22) showed a reduction in mental energy at each consecutive session compared to the previous session and to the control (both p≤0.05). A condition x pre-post interaction (F(1,23)=5.79, p=0.025, ηp 2 =0.20) indicates lower mental energy post-exercise compared to pre-exercise (p=0.009) and control (p=0.002), irrespective of day or session (figure 4c).

div.head: Mental fatigue
div.p: A condition x day interaction (F(1,23)=11.16, p=0.003, ηp 2 =0.33) found mental fatigue to be higher on day 2 in the exercise condition compared to day 1 (p=0.003) and the control on both days (p≤0.02). A condition x session interaction (F(2,46)= 5.99, p=0.005, ηp 2 =0.21) revealed higher mental fatigue at session 3 compared to 1 and 2 (both p<0.001) with mental fatigue at session 2 and 3 also being higher compared to control (both p<0.001). A condition x pre-post interaction (F(1,23)= 14.11, p=0.001, ηp 2 =0.38) found post-exercise ratings to be greater than preexercise (p<0.001) in the exercise condition, while ratings in the control condition were lower at both pre and post (both p<0.005) each session (See figure 4d).

div.head: Discussion
div.p: The aim of this study was to investigate the effect of repeated high-intensity sprint interval exercise on cognitive function, specifically reaction time and executive function as well as mood and perceptions of energy and fatigue. In support of the first hypothesis, we observed worse cognitive performance and mood on day 2, compared to day 1, attributed to a cumulative effect of fatigue from multiple HIIE bouts. More specifically, detrimental effects on inhibition and four choice rection time were observed, with an increase in errors (assessed by response accuracy on the Stroop task) and a slowing of RT (assessed on the FCRT) found on day 2. Within the mood measures, both alertness and contentedness were lower on day 2, alongside lower mental and physical energy and greater mental and physical fatigue. The second hypothesis stating that there would be a compounding effect of exercise-induced fatigue on cognitive function post-exercise, compared to pre-exercise, with the effect increasing across day 1 into day 2 was rejected, though detrimental effects on FCRT and visuo-spatial working memory were observed post-exercise following the later sessions in the day.

The results observed in the current study support within-match observations of reductions in technical skill and performance (Rampinini et al., 2009), in addition to reductions in performance across congested matches and tournaments (Ronglan et al., 2006). In contrast, Tsukamoto and colleagues (2016) found improved performance on the incongruent condition of the Stroop task following repeated HIIE. A possible explanation for the conflicting results when compared to those of Tsukamoto et al. (2016) is that whilst high-intensity exercise was implemented in their study (90 % V ̇O2max), the exercise was not at maximal effort and was on a cycle ergometer which has been shown to be a less cognitively demanding activity as compared to running, potentially due to requiring less metabolic energy (Lambourne & Tomporowski, 2010). Furthermore, the detrimental effects observed in the current study were driven by effects on day 2, which were not assessed by Tsukamoto et al. (2016). Higham and colleagues (2012) also observed different findings to the current study. When assessing movement patterns in rugby sevens, there was little indication of cumulative fatigue in players when comparing first and last games during tournaments that were held across two-days and consisted of five to six matches. This study, however, only reported movement patterns without skill, perceptual responses or any markers indicative of cognitive performance such as decision-making or missed passes and thus, the true impact of successive matches and residual fatigue was not fully assessed. What's more, these results might have been representative of effective between-match physiological recovery strategies rather than the effect of exercise-induced fatigue on consecutive performance.

Though the relationship between cognitive processes and sport-specific cognitive processes is unclear, it is interesting that the data from the current study supports previous findings of a reduction in sport-specific decision-making and accuracy, as assessed by the Loughborough Soccer Passing Test, across a 4-day soccer tournament (Sinclair & Artis, 2013). Our results found a reduction in accuracy on a more challenging cognitive task, with no effect on a simple task. This supports previous studies that have used intermittent protocols and found reductions in accuracy on both cognitive (Casanova et al., 2013) and performance-specific (Stone & Oliver, 2009) tasks. This might suggest that the effects of cumulative HIIE bouts have a greater impact on complex cognitive tasks that require the integration of different types of knowledge and skills to be applied in cognitively demanding circumstance. This is particularly pertinent to sport as complex cognitive skills, such as executive function, form the basis of sporting performance, being responsible for player decision making, response inhibition and cognitive flexibility. The importance of executive function in sport, and particularly that of strategic sports, like rugby, where athletes must adapt to highly varying situations including teammates, opponents, field position, and the environment, was highlighted recently by Krenn, Finkenzeller, Würth, and Amesberger (2018), and previously by Vestberg, Gustafson, Maurex, Ingvar, and Petrovic (2012) who found executive function was able to predict the success of top soccer players.

The ramifications of residual fatigue and impaired decision-making processes combined, both of which are synonymous with sports participation, extend beyond reductions in performance and can increase acute and long-term injury rates (Bengtsson, Ekstrand, & Hägglund, 2013;Borotikar, Newcomer, Koppes, & McLean, 2008). For example, fatigue and decision-making have been found to contribute to anterior cruciate ligament injury risk via the promotion of highrisk joint neuromechanical movement strategies during dynamic sports landings (Borotikar et al., 2008). As fatigue and decision-making rarely exist independent of one another in a real match environment, it may be that their combined manifestation presents as a worst-case scenario for injury risk, highlighting a need to monitor fatigue levels and cognitive processes, such as decision-making, to support player well-being.

Several theories may explain the results observed. Perhaps one of the most recent is the RAH model (Audiffren, 2016) which posits that acute exercise forces the brain to shift metabolic resources away from specific regions, such as the prefrontal cortex (PFC) responsible for highorder cognitive thinking, to instead favour structures that support exercise, such as the reticular formation and motor cortices. This process would consequently favour sensory and motor tasks whilst temporarily impairing executive function (Fontes et al., 2020;Moreau & Chou, 2019).

Following this theory, it could be suggested that greater resource was required in the motor cortex on day 2 due to increased fatigue, consequently reducing resource availability in the PFC and inhibiting cognitive performance. A neurochemical approach has also been suggested.

Initially proposed by (Cooper, 1973) and extended by (McMorris et al., 2016), this theory acknowledges the cascading effect exercise has on many neurochemicals in the brain, including catecholamines noradrenaline and dopamine, as well as cortisol, BDNF, and possibly serotonin.

Excessive concentrations of these neurochemicals, as a result of high-intensity or prolonged exercise, can inhibit cognitive function (McMorris et al., 2016) and may contribute to the effects observed. The application of both these theories to post-exercise cognitive effects, as opposed to during exercise, however, is still not clear and thus further investigation using brain imaging techniques such as electroencephalography or near-infrared spectroscopy is required.

Previous studies investigating repeated HIIE and executive function have associated reduced lactate levels with reduced executive functioning. As lactate is a main energy source for the brain during high-intensity activity, lower lactate levels may not adequately support neuronal activity and metabolism (Tsukamoto et al., 2016). Despite lower lactate levels reported here across HIIE sessions however, blood lactate levels remained relatively high on day 2 suggesting this is not a causal factor on its own. Indeed, reduced lactate levels suggest reduced glycolysis, which is a consequence of fatigue. A study found multiple short sprints, similar to that in the current study, resulted in neuromuscular fatigue (Fiorenza et al., 2019), which may reduce parasympathetic tone and attenuate executive functioning (Dupuy et al., 2018). Together with the slowing of sprint performance and self-rated increases in fatigue, the decline in cognitive and physical performance observed on day 2 of the current protocol might be attributed to both mental and physical fatigue, which is understood to be due to both peripheral and central mechanisms (Thomas et al., 2015). This is further supported by Barnes and Van Dyne (2009) who suggest physical and emotional fatigue may impair cognitive performance at high exercise intensities.

The increase in self-reported mental fatigue following cumulative high-intensity sprint sessions is particularly interesting. Over the last decade there has been a surge in research showing negative effects of mental fatigue on physical and sport-specific performance (Habay et al., 2021;Russell, Jenkins, Smith, Halson, & Kelly, 2019;Van Cutsem et al., 2017). Furthermore, mental fatigue has been found to affect underlying neural mechanisms, that being the activation and suppression of neural activity, of both physical (Tanaka, Ishii, & Watanabe, 2014) and cognitive (Tanaka, Ishii, & Watanabe, 2015) performance. Taking this into consideration, it is plausible that the increase in mental fatigue observed on day 2 might have contributed to the reductions in day 2 physical and cognitive performance. Interestingly, unlike all other studies, the current investigation did not purposefully impose an arduous mentally fatiguing task but despite this, perhaps unexpectedly, increases in mental fatigue appeared to be elicited by the cumulative high-intensity physical exercise sessions. This is supported by the finding that mental fatigue was not only greater on day 2 but increased following each individual sprint session and became increasingly greater session-to-session, illustrating a cumulative negative effect. This unique finding has important implications for sports involving congested fixture periods and warrants further examination, particularly in the absence of sufficient recovery. Indeed, the need for investigation into the potential cumulative effect of mental fatigue on performance over a competitive season has been recently highlighted (Russell et al., 2019). This study offers a first insight into this and posits that strenuous physical exercise itself may contribute to increases in mental fatigue, which might have effects cognitive as well as physical performance. Further studies are required in ecologically valid setting to extend this area of research and determine if similar results are observed in a real-world scenario.

Physical fitness and athletic experience have been reported to moderate cognitive performance following exercise (Chang et al., 2012;Voss et al., 2010). Highly trained sports players are able to sustain performance and recover more quickly from high-intensity intermittent exercise (Edwards, Macfadyen, & Clark, 2003); however, it is known that even well-trained games players become fatigued with increasing game time (Rampinini et al., 2009). As such, the fatigue induced via cumulative exercise bouts in the current study might have exceeded a functional threshold, even for trained players, resulting in the reported detrimental effects on cognitive performance and mood. As most research investigates the effects of exercise during or immediately following one acute exercise session, the present study highlights important implications for individuals involved in repeated exercise bouts within an intensified period. A significant increase in sprint time alongside reductions in HR and blood lactate values were observed on day 2 despite no change in effort, as shown by RPE, indicating the presence of exercise-induced fatigue. Reductions in mood, particularly alertness, and increases in mental fatigue were also observed on day 2. Research examining the effect of high-intensity exercise on mood is limited, particularly in trained populations. It has been suggested that trained individuals who are accustomed to strenuous exercise will find high-intensity exercise less aversive than their untrained counterparts and are more positive during exercise at higher intensities than less active individuals (Browne et al., 2017). The present study, however, is in support of others that have reported detrimental effects of multiday consecutive performance on mood, perceptions of fatigue and well-being (Gescheit et al., 2015). Unlike alertness and contentedness in which progressively negative effects of HIIE were observed, exercise had positive effects on calmness initially, but following one day of HIIE, negative effects were observed on day 2. Self-report measures of well-being and physical status are often overlooked in favour of objective physiological and biochemical markers; recent evidence however, demonstrates subjective measures are often more responsive to training and provide superior sensitivity and consistency compared to objective counterparts (Saw, Main, & Gastin, 2015). As self-report measures are easy to employ, quick to determine and physically non-invasive, they can provide a good indication of immediate player performance and wellbeing; something that could be of great use during congested periods of competition.

div.head: Limitations
div.p: The present study has several limitations. Firstly, only self-report was used to assess concussion history within the preceding 3 months prior to participation as well as prior to each testing session. Rugby players are at a higher risk for cognitive decline due to concussion history; to better qualify that participants had not experienced concussion close to testing, a validated questionnaire, such as the sport concussion assessment tool, would provide greater certainty over this variable that could influence the effect of exercise on cognitive function.

Secondly, the present study did not assess post-prandial glucose; pharmacological studies indicate that a low blood glucose level is associated with the release of counter-regulatory hormones such as cortisol, and an impairment in cerebral function. Though the influence of this mediator on cognitive performance has not been fully established, the assessment of blood glucose levels following each exercise bout may have provided greater insight into the potential reasoning behind the reductions observed across day 2.

It is also acknowledged that laboratory-based exercise does not replicate the demands of match performance and thus the present results have low ecological validity. Laboratory-based protocols do, however, enable greater control over confounding variables. The current protocol was selected based upon a sport-specific intermittent paradigm and was adapted from studies that have used sprint-based protocols (Howatson & Milak, 2009) in sporting populations (Austin et al., 2013). The task specificity was an important part of the protocol because meta-analytic assessment has demonstrated that the cognitive response may not be similar between exercise modes (Lambourne & Tomporowski, 2010). Furthermore, it has been shown that familiarity of exercise mode and exercise modality preference influence mood (Daley & Maynard, 2003) and brain cortical activity which may contribute to altered psychophysiological response (Brümmer, Schneider, Abel, Vogt, & Strueder, 2011). Thus, the use of a high-intensity sprint interval protocol in the present study could better reflect cognitive performance in intermittent team sports players, but further studies are needed to understand the effect of congested exercise on cognitive performance in a true sporting scenario.

div.head: Practical Applications & Conclusion
div.p: The present study found no effect of one days congested high-intensity sprint interval exercise on cognitive or physical performance. Compared to the first day however, reductions in inhibition, as assessed via the Stroop Task, energy and alertness alongside increases physical and mental fatigue were observed across a second day of congested exercise, in addition to reduced sprint performance and increases in perceived exertion. The results of this study therefore suggest that during congested exercise, such as congested tournament fixtures, increased consideration should be given for both performance outcomes and player well-being, though more research in this area is required to support these results and better understand the underlying mechanisms.

These results highlight several practical applications that may serve to help with performance success and player well-being. Perhaps most importantly is the need for cognitive recovery strategies in-between games and tournament days to ensure players have cognitively recovered. Furthermore, the importance of nutritional and physiological recovery strategies to minimise fatigue and increase alertness are of great importance.
The current work can be applied to many sports that involve congested tournaments, particularly those that encompass high-intensity sprint intervals, as well as applied professional occupations.

For example, military and emergency response personnel are frequently required to perform operations over several days that require the maintenance of cognitive performance under stressful conditions and high physical demands. Further understanding of the impact of these situations on both fundamental and task-specific cognitive processes is crucial for improving sports performance over prolonged periods of time and under strenuous exertion. Understanding the behavioural responses to congested HIIE and the behind them will work towards identification and development of intervention strategies to attenuate negative responses and prepare both the body and mind for each exercise session, thus enabling optimum performance during training and competition. This could, for example, involve player rotation, cognitive screening and priming, nutrition and recovery strategies.

figure.head: Figure 1
figure.figDesc: Figure 1 Schematic of testing protocol across one day in the a. experimental and b. control condition. The same protocol was replicated on day two in each respective condition.

The 12-item state component of the Mental and Physical State and Trait Energy and Fatigue Scale (Cronbach`s α = 0.89-0.91)(O'Connor, 2006), delivered via paper-and-pen, was used to assess four energy and fatigue mood states: Physical Energy State, Physical Fatigue State, Mental Energy State, and Mental Fatigue State. Participants were asked to indicate, "How do you feel right now with regard to your capacity to perform your typical physical activities" for the physical energy and fatigue questions. The instructions were identical for the mental energy and fatigue questions except that the focus was changed to "typical mental activities". The scales ranged from 'I feel I have no' to 'strongest feelings of…ever felt' with the adjectives at the end of each scale being energy/ fatigue/ vigour/ exhaustion/ pep/ feelings of being worn out.Participants rated the intensity of their current feelings by marking a point on each of the twelve presented 100 mm VAS. Each VAS item was scored 0-100 in mm from left to right along the horizontal line with 0 representing the lowest possible score and 100 being the highest.

figure.head: Figure 2
figure.figDesc: Figure 2 a. Simple reaction time (n=23); b. Four-choice reaction time (n=21); c. Corsi blocks

figure.head: Figure 3
figure.figDesc: Figure 3 a. Alertness b. contentedness c. calmness (all n=24) pre and post each session of high-

figure.head: Figure 4
figure.figDesc: Figure 4 a. Physical energy b. physical fatigue, c. Mental energy and d. mental fatigue (all n=24)

figure.head: Table 1
figure.figDesc: Average sprint time, HR, RPE and post-exercise blood lactate concentration following each session of repeated sprints on day 1 and 2 Data presented as mean ± SD. # Significant difference between sessions (p<0.05). ‡ Significant difference between days (p<0.05). HRav = average heart rate; BLa = blood lactate concentration; RPE = rate of perceived exertion.

div.head: Acknowledgements
div.p: The authors would like to express their gratitude to all participants for their enthusiasm and commitment. This work was supported by the Doctoral Collaborative Studentship Scheme at Northumbria University, UK.

div.head: Declaration of interest
div.p: There is no conflict of interest.

analytic.title: The effects of mental fatigue on physical performance: a systematic review

biblStruct.note: Van Cutsem, J., Marcora, S., De Pauw, K., Bailey, S., Meeusen, R., & Roelands, B. (2017). The effects of mental fatigue on physical performance: a systematic review. Sports Medicine, 1-20. doi:https://doi.org/10.1007/s40279-016-0672-0

analytic.title: Executive functions predict the success of top-soccer players

biblStruct.note: Vestberg, T., Gustafson, R., Maurex, L., Ingvar, M., & Petrovic, P. (2012). Executive functions predict the success of top-soccer players. PloS one, 7(4), e34731.

analytic.title: Are expert athletes 'expert'in the cognitive laboratory? A meta-analytic review of cognition and sport expertise

biblStruct.note: Voss, M. W., Kramer, A. F., Basak, C., Prakash, R. S., & Roberts, B. (2010). Are expert athletes 'expert'in the cognitive laboratory? A meta-analytic review of cognition and sport expertise. Applied Cognitive Psychology, 24(6), 812-826. doi:https://doi.org/10.1002/acp.1588

analytic.title: Executive function during acute exercise: The role of exercise intensity

biblStruct.note: Wang, C.-C., Chu, C.-H., Chu, I. H., Chan, K.-H., & Chang, Y.-K. (2013). Executive function during acute exercise: The role of exercise intensity. Journal of sport & exercise psychology, 35(4), 358-367.
